does: This paper presents Rewind-to-Delete, the first first-order black-box algorithm for certified machine unlearning on general nonconvex loss functions. The core method rewinds the model to an earlier checkpoint during training, then performs gradient descent on the remaining data, thereby efficiently removing information about deleted data points without full retraining. The authors prove (ε, δ)-differential privacy guarantees that certify the extent of data removal and establish privacy-utility-complexity tradeoffs. They additionally provide performance and generalization guarantees for functions satisfying the Polyak-Łojasiewicz condition, demonstrating that the unlearned model maintains competitive accuracy on retained data while provably forgetting deleted examples.
connects: Prior work on certified unlearning has primarily focused on convex or strongly convex objectives, where retraining complexity is tractable. This paper extends certified unlearning to the nonconvex setting, addressing a significant gap since modern deep learning operates almost entirely in nonconvex regimes. The approach builds on foundational differential privacy literature, applying its quantitative guarantees to the unlearning problem. It relates to machine unlearning literature that seeks efficient deletion without full retraining, and connects to recent advances in understanding nonconvex optimization under assumptions like the Polyak-Łojasiewicz condition, which characterizes a broad class of practical learning objectives.
enables: This work enables practical deployment of certified unlearning in real-world machine learning systems where regulatory requirements like the GDPR right to be forgotten must be satisfied with formal privacy guarantees. The black-box nature of the algorithm allows it to apply to any model trained with standard gradient descent, facilitating integration into existing pipelines. Future research can build on this foundation by tightening the privacy-utility-complexity tradeoffs, extending guarantees to other nonconvex settings beyond Polyak-Łojasiewicz functions, or developing more efficient rewind mechanisms that minimize performance degradation while maintaining certification strength.
